summary: Graham argues that the hardest startup lessons are counterintuitive: ship a minimal version quickly, then iterate relentlessly based on real user feedback. Fast releases expose flaws early, create urgency, and prevent founders from guessing what users want. Continuous improvement and user-centric design are the only durable advantages a startup can build.
key_points: Ship a minimal v1 quickly to learn from real users and expose fatal issues early.
Relentlessly add small improvements to keep momentum and signal progress to users.
User feedback beats founder intuition; design for casual visitors first.
Delighting users creates loyalty and organic growth without heavy marketing.
money_quotes: I've seen a lot of startups die because they were too slow to release stuff, and none because they were too quick.
Users hate bugs, but they don't seem to mind a minimal version 1, if there's more coming soon.
If your product seems finished, there are two possible explanations: (a) it is finished, or (b) you lack imagination.
A startup has to sing for its supper.
The median visitor will arrive with their finger poised on the Back button.
key_insight: Startups win by shipping early, iterating fast, and obsessing over user happiness instead of guessing in private.
